paragraphs: This paper leverages technological and methodological advances in natural language processing to advance our understanding and approaches to leadership research by introducing structural topic models (STM) to researchers wanting to inductively code massive amounts of unstructured texts. Specifically, we illustrate the application of STM applied to a large corpus (N ≈ 8000) of unstructured text responses from a diverse sample of leaders to inductively generate a classification system of leader challenges and simultaneously examine whether the challenges being experienced by leaders covary with leader characteristics. Overall, we identify nine central leader challenges. Results indicate that certain leader challenges are more prevalent depending on the leader's characteristics (e.g., gender), and that two challenges, Daily Management Activities and Communication, were significantly related to boss' ratings of performance. We also highlight additional applications of this technique to aid leadership researchers who wish to inductively derive meaning from large amounts of unstructured texts.

Leadership research has entered a digital era where the study of leadership has collided with rapid advances in technology. Advances in computer science in areas such as natural language processing, machine learning, and artificial intelligence, along with an abundance of data from big data sources (e.g., unstructured text, social media, videos, location tracking) presents unique opportunities to propel the scholarship of leadership forward. The purpose of this paper is to aid in that effort by introducing structural topic models (STM), a natural language processing (NLP) approach that utilizes unsupervised machine learning to extract topics from textual data (Roberts et al., 2019), to leadership researchers who may be faced with the daunting task of inductively coding massive amounts of unstructured text.

Specifically, we describe the step-by-step application of structural topic models (STM) to inductively mine text. Our text corpus comes from a large sample (N ≈ 8000) of leaders who provided unstructured text responses about the most significant challenges they face as a leader or manager that was collected as part of a larger leader development program. STM enables us to inductively develop a classification system of challenges from a large and diverse sample of leaders and simultaneously examine whether challenges experienced by leaders covary with leader characteristics and whether they relate to performance outcomes. STM permits the inclusion of document metadata, which is information about the document itself such as who authored it and when. In this example, data is available about the characteristics of the leaders who authored the documents. Via STM, we can leverage this data to gain an understanding of whether certain leaders are more likely to report certain challenges (i.e., topic prevalence) as well as how the content of particular challenges might vary across different leaders. In summary, using STM, we simultaneously examine what challenges leaders face, their cooccurrence with other challenges, and their relationship with leader attributes and performance outcomes.

We also aim to contribute to the practice of leader development with our results informing practitioners of the specific challenges leaders face. Mining such a large corpus of 8000+ managers' expressed challenges via their exact words can better inform training and development practitioners of the substance, design, and delivery of leader development to maximize relevance of the content for leaders taking part in such initiatives. Thus, our paper answers the call of this special issue by leveraging an unsupervised machine learning approach to gain insights into leadership. Our primary focus is to illustrate and discuss how to apply STM to leadership and leader development research and practice by using leader challenges data as an example. In addition, we detail a robust discussion of different underlying purposes of STM with examples to leader development research, thus highlighting the ways recent technological and methodological advances in big data methods such as machine learning and natural language processing can advance our understanding of leadership.

sections.paper_id: Tonidandel et al. - 2022 - Using structural topic modeling to gain insight into challenges faced by leaders
paragraphs: Researchers are likely quite familiar with qualitative text analysis as a method for inductive inquiry. Qualitative approaches such as grounded theory or content analysis are extremely useful for inductively generating a coherent and comprehensive understanding of a phenomenon (Duriau et al., 2007;Krippendorff, 2018;Turner, 1983). However, such approaches are resource intensive and do not scale well. With advances in computational power and efficiency, tools for mining large amounts of unstructured text data are becoming widely available (Campion & Campion, 2020). Historically, when automating the quantification and coding of a large amount of unstructured text, researchers have relied on computeraided-text-analysis (CATA; Short et al., 2018;Short & Palmer, 2008). CATA can use pre-existing dictionaries to investigate the prevalence of particular words in a corpus of text. A similar approach is to eschew the dictionary in favor of counting the frequency of all words. These frequency-based approaches use what is commonly referred to as a "bag of words" (BOW) paradigm in which each word represents a column in the data set (Harris, 1954). At their most basic level, these tools count the occurrence of particular words in a corpus of text. In addition to numerical counts, visualizations such as word clouds can depict the prevalence of words, and one can examine the relationship between word prevalence and outcomes of interest (Zhang et al., 2010).

Advances in computer science have helped propel the mining of text data beyond simple BOW using NLP and tools such as topic modeling. Topic models are an automated method for content analyzing large amounts of text and are used in applications where the main task is keyword extraction (Albalawi et al., 2020). Unlike simpler frequency-based methods, topic models use unsupervised machine learning techniques to impose structure on the text. Since they are unsupervised, one does not need a predefined set of coding criteria. Rather, the structure is imposed inductively by the algorithm. The way structure is achieved is by considering not only the frequency of individual words, but also the association among different words and the association among documents (Wallach, 2006). Topic models utilize the distributional hypothesis of linguistics (Firth, 1957;Harris, 1954) to infer a word's meaning based upon its co-occurrence with other words. The central premise underlying topic models is that words that occur in a similar context tend to have similar meanings. In this manner, different words can be identified as referencing a related underlying concept because those words all appear in a similar context and thus have similar relationships to other words. For example, Xie et al. (2015) conducted a topic model analysis of newsgroups. They found words like 'guns,' 'police,' and 'criminal' make up a topic they labeled 'crime', and words like 'game,' 'season,' and 'player,' make up a topic they labeled 'sports.' Not only can topic models automatically identify distinct words that should be grouped together because they share a similar meaning, topic models can also differentiate between identical words that may take on different meanings, called polysemy. A common example of polysemy is the word mole that could refer to the small burrowing animal, a skin blemish, or even a spy planted to steal secrets (McCaughren, 2009). A traditional CATA approach would count the number of instances the word mole appeared in a text, but it would not be able to differentiate between these different usage cases. In contrast, topic models, by examining the word's co-occurrence with other words, can differentiate between meanings and this identical word can be correctly classified into different topics depending on its particular context. Multiple examples of this approach have begun to appear in the organizational science literature and topic models have been shown to classify documents reliably and accurately to topics (Banks et al., 2018;Doldor et al., 2019;Schmiedel et al., 2019).

Topic models can be used to answer a series of critical questions about a corpus of text. First, topic models can identify the number of topics being discussed. Second, topic models can identify the key terms that comprise particular topics. Using those key terms, one can begin to define or label the various topics identified. Finally, topic models can also be used to assign documents to particular topics. Parallels can be seen between topic modeling and exploratory factor analysis (EFA). In EFA, one attempts to take the quantitative scores generated from a series of items to understand how many latent constructs are being assessed by these items and which items are assessing which constructs. In a similar vein, topic models attempt to use word frequencies and their associated distributional properties to understand how many topics are being discussed by these words and which words correspond to which topics. Like EFA, where items can load onto multiple constructs, in topic modeling, words can contribute to multiple topics and documents can discuss multiple topics. Despite the algorithmic nature of topic modeling, subjective judgement still plays a role in defining/naming the topics that are identified, which is similar to the process of naming latent constructs in EFA.

Structural topic models (STM) take traditional topic models one-step further by incorporating document metadata into the topic estimation. In the present study, document metadata consists of information about the leader such as gender, race, citizenship, organizational level, and job performance. The inclusion of this additional data typically leads to better model fit and also allows one to explore relationships between metadata and topics. STM, when applied to a large number of unstructured text responses, allows one to inductively generate a classification of topics and examine how both the prevalence of topics and their associated content may vary as a function of the characteristics of participants. STM shares similarities with Bayesian inference. The STM process starts by creating an initial estimate of topic content and prevalence. Those initial values get updated in an iterative process based on the data until a solution converges which is evaluated according to how likely that solution is given the data. Hannigan et al. (2019) point to topic modeling as a fruitful approach to advancing management theory in five primary ways: detecting novelty and emergence, developing inductive classification systems, understanding online audiences and products, analyzing frames and social movements, and understanding cultural dynamics. To illustrate topic modeling applied to leadership research, we apply this approach to develop an inductive classification system of leader challenges.

sections.paper_id: Tonidandel et al. - 2022 - Using structural topic modeling to gain insight into challenges faced by leaders
paragraphs: No doubt, leader development is seen as a crucial driver of organizational effectiveness. As a result, estimates of annual corporate expenditures on leader development are as high as $50 billion with costs ranging between $4000 and $10,000 per person each year for many organizations (Prokopeak, 2018). Still, it seems to be just as difficult to develop leaders now than it ever has due to three different gaps: (1) a gap in motivation between organizations and individual participants; (2) a gap between the skills programs build and what organizations actually want; and (3) a gap in transferring what is learned in the classroom to one's job (Moldoveanu & Narayandas, 2019). Given these gaps, there is no doubt researchers and practitioners find themselves in a digital era that may change the way leader development operates and how these phenomena are observed, studied, and researched. In turn, we believe research in leader development has not fully leveraged recent technological and methodological advances to better understand and fill the aforementioned gaps in leader development.

Theoretically, the more leader development is framed around the actual needs of participants of development initiatives, the better the outcomes for participants and organizations. If one were to neglect a needs analysis, the outcome could be filled with generic and nonspecific content that does not explicitly meet the demands and challenges of the leaders participating in the program (Collins & Holton, 2004). There is a long history of the importance of a needs analysis in training and development (Arthur et al., 2003;Baldwin & Ford, 1988;Blume et al., 2010;Moore & Dutton, 1978). In one of the earliest, seminal pieces of work in the area of training and development, McGehee and Thayer (1961) proposed three areas of analysis to ensure a systematic, purposeful determination of training needs thus maximizing the transfer of learning: organization (where in the organization training should be used), operational (what should be taught) and person (how well one is performing). We aim to enhance and expand specifically on the operational aspect, thus making a theoretical and methodological contribution by utilizing a new methodological approach to better inform the literature on needs analysis in leader training and development.

Past research has stressed the need to understand participants' expectations prior to training and development activities (Bouloutian, 2009;Tannenbaum et al., 1991). Further, when participants understand the purpose of the training, they have higher program satisfaction upon completion of the initiative (Ardts, van der Velde, & Maurer, 2010). If participants attend programs that do not address their specific leader challenges, they are likely to view the experience as irrelevant and a waste of precious time. Consequently, determining the challenges participants face as leaders is important for aiding in the design and delivery of training content.

To answer that call, recent research has uncovered certain challenges of specific populations of leaders. For instance, Gentry et al. (2014) gathered data from 763 leaders from seven different countries who participated in a leadership development program targeted to middle-to-senior-level managers. Through thematic analysis (Boyatzis, 1998), they developed a coding rubric on a sample of N = 100 leader challenge documents and subsequently applied that rubric to classify the challenges faced by other leaders. They discovered cross-cultural similarities in challenges of middle-to-senior-level managers despite geographical and cultural differences; Developing Managerial Effectiveness, Inspiring Others, Developing Employees, Leading a Team, Guiding Change, and Managing Internal Stakeholders were challenges leaders faced similarly around the world (Gentry et al., 2014).

A deeper understanding of the types of challenges leaders face on the job can indeed enhance the developmental opportunities leaders receive and better prepare them for the specific issues they are dealing with or will encounter on the job. Although Gentry et al. (2014) presented an initial typology of leader challenges at the middle-tosenior levels of management, it was generated from a limited evaluation of a small sample of leaders (N = 100) to form a codebook that was then used for the other managers in the study. Unfortunately, any large-scale exploration of the types of challenges faced by leaders is difficult due to the nature of this kind of datatypically unstructured text.

It remains to be seen whether the same challenges would be present in a larger, more diverse sample of leaders. STM allows us to address this issue directly via its ability to examine a large amount of unstructured text and derive the underlying topics. Furthermore, STM enables the discovery of new knowledge by incorporating leaders' "features" or personal characteristics such as race, gender, and managerial level in the topic generation process. Later on, we provide examples of future applica-tions to show how topic modeling approaches can be useful for advancing our knowledge of leadership in a variety of domains.
sections.text:
No doubt, leader development is seen as a crucial driver of organizational effectiveness. As a result, estimates of annual corporate expenditures on leader development are as high as $50 billion with costs ranging between $4000 and $10,000 per person each year for many organizations (Prokopeak, 2018). Still, it seems to be just as difficult to develop leaders now than it ever has due to three different gaps: (1) a gap in motivation between organizations and individual participants; (2) a gap between the skills programs build and what organizations actually want; and (3) a gap in transferring what is learned in the classroom to one's job (Moldoveanu & Narayandas, 2019). Given these gaps, there is no doubt researchers and practitioners find themselves in a digital era that may change the way leader development operates and how these phenomena are observed, studied, and researched. In turn, we believe research in leader development has not fully leveraged recent technological and methodological advances to better understand and fill the aforementioned gaps in leader development.

Theoretically, the more leader development is framed around the actual needs of participants of development initiatives, the better the outcomes for participants and organizations. If one were to neglect a needs analysis, the outcome could be filled with generic and nonspecific content that does not explicitly meet the demands and challenges of the leaders participating in the program (Collins & Holton, 2004). There is a long history of the importance of a needs analysis in training and development (Arthur et al., 2003;Baldwin & Ford, 1988;Blume et al., 2010;Moore & Dutton, 1978). In one of the earliest, seminal pieces of work in the area of training and development, McGehee and Thayer (1961) proposed three areas of analysis to ensure a systematic, purposeful determination of training needs thus maximizing the transfer of learning: organization (where in the organization training should be used), operational (what should be taught) and person (how well one is performing). We aim to enhance and expand specifically on the operational aspect, thus making a theoretical and methodological contribution by utilizing a new methodological approach to better inform the literature on needs analysis in leader training and development.

Past research has stressed the need to understand participants' expectations prior to training and development activities (Bouloutian, 2009;Tannenbaum et al., 1991). Further, when participants understand the purpose of the training, they have higher program satisfaction upon completion of the initiative (Ardts, van der Velde, & Maurer, 2010). If participants attend programs that do not address their specific leader challenges, they are likely to view the experience as irrelevant and a waste of precious time. Consequently, determining the challenges participants face as leaders is important for aiding in the design and delivery of training content.

To answer that call, recent research has uncovered certain challenges of specific populations of leaders. For instance, Gentry et al. (2014) gathered data from 763 leaders from seven different countries who participated in a leadership development program targeted to middle-to-senior-level managers. Through thematic analysis (Boyatzis, 1998), they developed a coding rubric on a sample of N = 100 leader challenge documents and subsequently applied that rubric to classify the challenges faced by other leaders. They discovered cross-cultural similarities in challenges of middle-to-senior-level managers despite geographical and cultural differences; Developing Managerial Effectiveness, Inspiring Others, Developing Employees, Leading a Team, Guiding Change, and Managing Internal Stakeholders were challenges leaders faced similarly around the world (Gentry et al., 2014).

A deeper understanding of the types of challenges leaders face on the job can indeed enhance the developmental opportunities leaders receive and better prepare them for the specific issues they are dealing with or will encounter on the job. Although Gentry et al. (2014) presented an initial typology of leader challenges at the middle-tosenior levels of management, it was generated from a limited evaluation of a small sample of leaders (N = 100) to form a codebook that was then used for the other managers in the study. Unfortunately, any large-scale exploration of the types of challenges faced by leaders is difficult due to the nature of this kind of datatypically unstructured text.

Although various methodologies exist for imposing structure on text data (e.g., grounded theory or content analysis), those methodologies do not scale well (Campion et al., 2016). It remains to be seen whether the same challenges would be present in a larger, more diverse sample of leaders. STM allows us to address this issue directly via its ability to examine a large amount of unstructured text and derive the underlying topics. Furthermore, STM enables the discovery of new knowledge by incorporating leaders' "features" or personal characteristics such as race, gender, and managerial level in the topic generation process. Later on, we provide examples of future applica-tions to show how topic modeling approaches can be useful for advancing our knowledge of leadership in a variety of domains.

sections.header_raw: An illustration of STM applied to leader challenges
sections.header_norm: an illustration of stm applied to leader challenges
paragraphs: In the present study, we demonstrate how the machine learning technique of STM can discover what challenges leaders face at scale, as well as their co-occurrence with other challenges and their relationship with leader attributes and performance. We thus use and apply recent technological and methodological advances in big data, NLP, and machine learning to a large corpus of text describing leader challenges in an effort to answer the following research questions and to contribute to leader development:

RQ1: What are the common challenges experienced by leaders? RQ2: Are certain challenges likely to co-occur and if so, which ones? RQ3: Are certain types of leaders more or less likely to experience certain types of challenges? RQ4: Are certain challenges associated with varying levels of job performance?

paragraphs: The initial sample consisted of 8414 managers who took a 360degree leadership assessment from a third-party leadership development provider prior to the actual start of the leadership development program. A detailed breakdown of the wide-range of business sectors represented by the participants is contained in Table 1. The program is a three-day, face-to-face program that focuses primarily on those new to leadership and leaders who are frontline, first-level, or midlevel managers or supervisors. The program usually has 2-3 trainers, typically ranging from 12 to 30 participants from around the world in a variety of for profit, nonprofit, government, and education industries and sectors. The program is geared to help experienced managers and supervisors of individual contributors, those new to leadership, and those who are seen as high potentials by their organization and have the aptitude to quickly advance up the organizational hierarchy. The program focuses on several themes and competencies (e.g., selfawareness, communication, influence, conflict).

We eliminated responses from 38 participants who did not report key demographic information used in the analysis like gender and organizational level. Responses from an additional 630 participants were removed because job performance ratings were unavailable. Finally, responses from 458 individuals were removed for failing to report leader challenges. The final sample consisted of 7288 leaders, with the majority being male (62.6%) and Caucasian (51.6%). However, it should be noted that race data was unavailable for 32.0% of the sample. These individuals were retained for the analyses but were assigned to a category of "not reported". Sixty-five percent of the sample were in a middle-management position, while twenty-five percent of the sample were front-line supervisors. The majority of the sample was based in the US (70.7%), and the mean age of participants was 40.09 (SD = 8.50).

paragraphs: Leader Challenges. Before the in-person leadership development program took place, each participant was required to fill out questions on a 360-degree leadership assessment. As part of their 360-degree leadership assessment, participants responded to an open-ended prompt "What are your most significant challenges as a leader/manager?" Participants were provided with three separate text boxes for their responses. Though individuals are encouraged to discuss what they view as their key challenges with their bosses, they are not required to show what they actually write in these text boxes. For the analyses, all responses were collapsed to make a single document for each individual. The number of words used to describe challenges ranged from a single word to 322 words, with the average length of these documents being 83.18 words (Median = 65). Descriptive statistics for document length as a function of demographic information known about the respondent is provided in Table 2.

Demographics. As part of the 360-degree assessment, participants provided a host of demographic information. For the purposes of this study, we used gender, organizational level (coded front-line, middle, executive, or not applicable), race (coded as Caucasian, African American, Hispanic, Other, or Not available/reported), and whether or not the participant was a citizen of the United States.

Job Performance. Job performance was assessed by a six-item measure completed by participants' direct supervisor. In rare instances, when more than one direct supervisor existed, we averaged across the two raters. Example items from this measure include "How would you rate this person's effectiveness in the organization?" and "Where would you place this person as a leader relative to other leaders in similar roles?" Responses were provided on a five-point scale with answers ranging from 1 = "Among the worst" to 5 = "Among the best". Internal consistency reliability for the six items was 0.92 as assessed by Cronbach's alpha.

sections.paper_id: Tonidandel et al. - 2022 - Using structural topic modeling to gain insight into challenges faced by leaders
paragraphs: Step 1 -Data Cleaning. Before running STM, several data preparation steps are needed. First, the individual challenge documents from each participant were subject to standard data preparation practices when mining text (Banks et al., 2018). Here we converted all text to lowercase and removed punctuation, numbers, and symbols. In addition, we removed stop words. Stop words are common words in a language that do not add much meaning to a sentence. Examples include words such as "the", "a", "by", and "so". Because stop words both appear frequently in text, yet convey little meaning, their removal improves the performance of language classification algorithms like STM. There is no standard dictionary of stop words, but numerous stop word libraries exist. For this application, we used the stop word dictionary in R found in the quanteda package (Benoit et al., 2018). In addition to the conventional stop word list, three custom stop words were added to the stop word library: leadership, challenge, and challenges. Because these words were part of the prompt, many participants began their responses with something like "One leadership challenge I have is…". As a result, we removed this source of noise from the text documents.

Other popular text normalization techniques include stemming and lemmatization. Both techniques reduce words to their roots (Banks et al., 2018). Stemming leverages patterns to determine the stem without accounting for vocabulary, context, or parts-of-speech. In contrast, lemmatization involves a morphological analysis of the words to extract the root word (Singh & Gupta, 2017). Research shows mixed findings regarding the added value of stemming and lemmatization processes when applied to large datasets (Banks et al., 2018). Furthermore, evidence suggests the incorporation of these approaches may diminish the performance of topic modeling techniques (Schofield & Mimno, 2016). As a result, we elected to not use stemming or lemmatization processes in the data preparation stage.

Step 2 -Data Preparation. The cleaned leader challenge texts were transformed into a conventional word-document matrix. In such a matrix, each row of data represents the text provided by each participant (i.e., document) and each column of data is used to signify each word used across an entire text corpus. In addition to assigning individual words to a column of data (unigrams), we also used two-word blocks (bi-grams) in our analyses. The decision to use unigrams, bigrams, or even tri-grams, reflects the need to strike a balance between sufficiently capturing meaning without unnecessarily increasing model complexity. As one progresses from unigrams to bi-grams to tri-grams and beyond, the number of columns in the data set increases exponentially producing an extremely sparse high dimensional data set. Unfortunately, high dimensional data is fraught with a number of analytical challenges that preclude the application of numerous kinds of analytic techniques, known as the curse of dimensionality (Bellman, 1961). Although, multi-grams should be avoided for this reason, one may wish to include them if there is reason to believe that multi-grams may convey important meaning beyond unigrams. In our case, we made an a priori decision to include bi-grams because we believed that many challenges might be reflected by two word units such as time management or work family. Because infrequent words contribute little to the understanding of common topics yet can increase the computational complexity of structural topic models, we used the common practice of setting a lower threshold for inclusion in the word-document matrix. For a word or bi-gram to be included, it must be present in more than five documents. Our final text corpus consisted of 7274 documents, 8353 terms and 300,947 tokens (accounting for both unigrams and bi-grams).

Step 3 -Conducting a Topic Model Analysis. Structural topic models were used to derive the underlying topics in this text corpus. We used the stm package (Roberts et al., 2019) in R (R Core Team, 2018) to conduct our analyses. STM is an extension of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA; Blei, Ng, & Jordon, 2003). LDA represents documents as Note. M and SD represent mean and standard deviation, respectively.

mixtures of topics that produce certain words. In LDA, a Dirichlet prior is used and the prevalence of a topic and the words used to discuss a certain topic are assumed to be constant across all documents. STM is an extension of the LDA model that incorporates features of a correlated topic model (Blei & Lafferty, 2007) and the sparse additive generative topic model (Eisenstein et al., 2011). In STM, documents are still assumed to arise from a mixture of topics, but correlations among topic proportions are permitted and both the prevalence of words and the distribution of topics within each document can be influenced by covariates.

Step 3a -Determine the number of topics. One of the first critical steps in STM is determining the number of topics that are represented in the text corpus. To investigate this issue, we used an iterative procedure where we first obtained a topic model solution for between 5 and 100 topics incrementing by 5 using the searchK() function. Next, we inspected both the cross-validation likelihood and semantic coherence to decide how many latent topics were in our corpus. The crossvalidation likelihood reflects the fit of each solution in a hold-out sample whereas semantic coherence is a measure of how often words in a topic co-occur. Fig. 1 contains a depiction of each metric across the various solutions and, when considering both these criteria, the optimal solution was at 10 topics (see Fig. 1). This process is akin to visual inspection of the scree plot in EFA. We then refined our topic search to explore all solutions between 5 and 15 topics incrementing by 1 and again visually inspected the cross-validation likelihood and semantic coherence results of each solution. We identified nine topics as the optimal solution based on these same two criteria. 1 Step 3b -Evaluate the quality of the solution. We continued to evaluate the suitability of our nine-topic solution using both quantitative and qualitative metrics. Topic quality can be assessed statistically by looking at each topic's semantic coherence (as opposed to the semantic coherence of the entire solution as was done above), the word similarity of the top words, and each topic's exclusivity, the amount of overlap in top words across topics. While most of the topics identified perform well according to these indices, Daily Management Activities scored high on exclusivity but somewhat lower on semantic coherence relative to the other topics. The opposite pattern was found for Executive Presence such that exclusivity was relatively lower than the other topics, but semantic coherence was high. The remaining seven topics all performed similarly on these two metrics. In general, these metrics continue to support the validity of the nine-topic solution.

Although quantitative measures such as coherence have been shown to be related to expert judgements of topic quality (Mimno et al., 2011), a critical next step is to evaluate the face validity of the solution (Schmiedel et al., 2019). To interpret the nine-topic solution, we first examined four common metrics used to evaluate the words associated with each topic (Roberts et al., 2019). Table 3 contains the results for our nine topics along with our user-generated labels for each topic. Highest probability contains the words within each topic with the highest frequency of occurrence. The remaining three metrics are all designed to identify words that are core to a particular topic and yet distinguish that topic from other topics. FREX weights words by how frequent words are in a particular topic and also by how exclusive they are to that particular topic. Lift is a similar metric that weights words by dividing by a word's frequency in other topics thereby giving higher weights to words that appear less frequently in other topics. Finally, score weights words by the log frequency of a word in a topic divided by the log frequency of the word in other topics. Further information about the central ideas being expressed in each topic can be found in the documents with the highest probability of belonging to a particular topic. Step 1 -Data Cleaning. Before running STM, several data preparation steps are needed. First, the individual challenge documents from each participant were subject to standard data preparation practices when mining text (Banks et al., 2018). Here we converted all text to lowercase and removed punctuation, numbers, and symbols. In addition, we removed stop words. Stop words are common words in a language that do not add much meaning to a sentence. Examples include words such as "the", "a", "by", and "so". Because stop words both appear frequently in text, yet convey little meaning, their removal improves the performance of language classification algorithms like STM. There is no standard dictionary of stop words, but numerous stop word libraries exist. For this application, we used the stop word dictionary in R found in the quanteda package (Benoit et al., 2018). In addition to the conventional stop word list, three custom stop words were added to the stop word library: leadership, challenge, and challenges. Because these words were part of the prompt, many participants began their responses with something like "One leadership challenge I have is…". As a result, we removed this source of noise from the text documents.

Other popular text normalization techniques include stemming and lemmatization. Both techniques reduce words to their roots (Banks et al., 2018). Stemming leverages patterns to determine the stem without accounting for vocabulary, context, or parts-of-speech. In contrast, lemmatization involves a morphological analysis of the words to extract the root word (Singh & Gupta, 2017). Research shows mixed findings regarding the added value of stemming and lemmatization processes when applied to large datasets (Banks et al., 2018). Furthermore, evidence suggests the incorporation of these approaches may diminish the performance of topic modeling techniques (Schofield & Mimno, 2016). As a result, we elected to not use stemming or lemmatization processes in the data preparation stage.

Step 2 -Data Preparation. The cleaned leader challenge texts were transformed into a conventional word-document matrix. In such a matrix, each row of data represents the text provided by each participant (i.e., document) and each column of data is used to signify each word used across an entire text corpus. In addition to assigning individual words to a column of data (unigrams), we also used two-word blocks (bi-grams) in our analyses. The decision to use unigrams, bigrams, or even tri-grams, reflects the need to strike a balance between sufficiently capturing meaning without unnecessarily increasing model complexity. As one progresses from unigrams to bi-grams to tri-grams and beyond, the number of columns in the data set increases exponentially producing an extremely sparse high dimensional data set. Unfortunately, high dimensional data is fraught with a number of analytical challenges that preclude the application of numerous kinds of analytic techniques, known as the curse of dimensionality (Bellman, 1961). Although, multi-grams should be avoided for this reason, one may wish to include them if there is reason to believe that multi-grams may convey important meaning beyond unigrams. In our case, we made an a priori decision to include bi-grams because we believed that many challenges might be reflected by two word units such as time management or work family. Because infrequent words contribute little to the understanding of common topics yet can increase the computational complexity of structural topic models, we used the common practice of setting a lower threshold for inclusion in the word-document matrix. For a word or bi-gram to be included, it must be present in more than five documents. Our final text corpus consisted of 7274 documents, 8353 terms and 300,947 tokens (accounting for both unigrams and bi-grams).

Step 3 -Conducting a Topic Model Analysis. Structural topic models were used to derive the underlying topics in this text corpus. We used the stm package (Roberts et al., 2019) in R (R Core Team, 2018) to conduct our analyses. STM is an extension of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA; Blei, Ng, & Jordon, 2003). LDA represents documents as Note. M and SD represent mean and standard deviation, respectively.

mixtures of topics that produce certain words. In LDA, a Dirichlet prior is used and the prevalence of a topic and the words used to discuss a certain topic are assumed to be constant across all documents. STM is an extension of the LDA model that incorporates features of a correlated topic model (Blei & Lafferty, 2007) and the sparse additive generative topic model (Eisenstein et al., 2011). In STM, documents are still assumed to arise from a mixture of topics, but correlations among topic proportions are permitted and both the prevalence of words and the distribution of topics within each document can be influenced by covariates.

Step 3a -Determine the number of topics. One of the first critical steps in STM is determining the number of topics that are represented in the text corpus. To investigate this issue, we used an iterative procedure where we first obtained a topic model solution for between 5 and 100 topics incrementing by 5 using the searchK() function. Next, we inspected both the cross-validation likelihood and semantic coherence to decide how many latent topics were in our corpus. The crossvalidation likelihood reflects the fit of each solution in a hold-out sample whereas semantic coherence is a measure of how often words in a topic co-occur. Fig. 1 contains a depiction of each metric across the various solutions and, when considering both these criteria, the optimal solution was at 10 topics (see Fig. 1). This process is akin to visual inspection of the scree plot in EFA. We then refined our topic search to explore all solutions between 5 and 15 topics incrementing by 1 and again visually inspected the cross-validation likelihood and semantic coherence results of each solution. We identified nine topics as the optimal solution based on these same two criteria. 1 Step 3b -Evaluate the quality of the solution. We continued to evaluate the suitability of our nine-topic solution using both quantitative and qualitative metrics. While most of the topics identified perform well according to these indices, Daily Management Activities scored high on exclusivity but somewhat lower on semantic coherence relative to the other topics. The opposite pattern was found for Executive Presence such that exclusivity was relatively lower than the other topics, but semantic coherence was high. The remaining seven topics all performed similarly on these two metrics. In general, these metrics continue to support the validity of the nine-topic solution.

To investigate this issue, we used an iterative procedure where we first obtained a topic model solution for between 5 and 100 topics incrementing by 5 using the searchK() function. The crossvalidation likelihood reflects the fit of each solution in a hold-out sample whereas semantic coherence is a measure of how often words in a topic co-occur. Fig. 1 contains a depiction of each metric across the various solutions and, when considering both these criteria, the optimal solution was at 10 topics (see Fig. 1). This process is akin to visual inspection of the scree plot in EFA. We then refined our topic search to explore all solutions between 5 and 15 topics incrementing by 1 and again visually inspected the cross-validation likelihood and semantic coherence results of each solution. We identified nine topics as the optimal solution based on these same two criteria. 1 Step 3b -Evaluate the quality of the solution. We continued to evaluate the suitability of our nine-topic solution using both quantitative and qualitative metrics. Topic quality can be assessed statistically by looking at each topic's semantic coherence (as opposed to the semantic coherence of the entire solution as was done above), the word similarity of the top words, and each topic's exclusivity, the amount of overlap in top words across topics. While most of the topics identified perform well according to these indices, Daily Management Activities scored high on exclusivity but somewhat lower on semantic coherence relative to the other topics. The opposite pattern was found for Executive Presence such that exclusivity was relatively lower than the other topics, but semantic coherence was high. The remaining seven topics all performed similarly on these two metrics. In general, these metrics continue to support the validity of the nine-topic solution.

Although quantitative measures such as coherence have been shown to be related to expert judgements of topic quality (Mimno et al., 2011), a critical next step is to evaluate the face validity of the solution (Schmiedel et al., 2019). To interpret the nine-topic solution, we first examined four common metrics used to evaluate the words associated with each topic (Roberts et al., 2019). Table 3 contains the results for our nine topics along with our user-generated labels for each topic. Highest probability contains the words within each topic with the highest frequency of occurrence. The remaining three metrics are all designed to identify words that are core to a particular topic and yet distinguish that topic from other topics. FREX weights words by how frequent words are in a particular topic and also by how exclusive they are to that particular topic. Lift is a similar metric that weights words by dividing by a word's frequency in other topics thereby giving higher weights to words that appear less frequently in other topics. Finally, score weights words by the log frequency of a word in a topic divided by the log frequency of the word in other topics. The top words identified by these metrics were used as a starting point for naming the topics. Further information about the central ideas being expressed in each topic can be found in the documents with the highest probability of belonging to a particular topic. Table 4 contains exemplars of the highest scoring documents in each topic. Using the information from Table 3 and Table 4, three subject matter experts, all PhD level scientistpractitioners in the leadership development domain with extensive experience publishing and applying leadership research, evaluated the top word list and top scoring documents and generated a label for each topic. These subject matter experts then exchanged labels and discussed discrepancies until all three subject matter experts were able to agree on a consensus label for each of the nine topics found in those tables. This naming process is consistent with best practices in topic modeling labeling (e.g., Banks et al., 2018;Schmiedel et al., 2019). Those consensus labels are used in the remainder of the manuscript.

Step 4 -Describe the solution: Topic models provide a wide variety of output that enable one to address a host of research questions. We have already engaged in one of the first key descriptive steps of using the topics to identify the categories of challenges being faced by leaders. Other common issues that can be examined using the topic model solution are the frequency of the various topics, how topics relate to one another, and predictors of topic frequency and prevalence. We address each of these in turn.

Topic prevalence. Topic prevalence is the proportion of that document that can be associated with a particular topic (Roberts et al., 2014). STM ascribes a topic prevalence score for every topic to each document. Using topic prevalence, one can examine how frequent various topics are appearing in the entire corpus. Fig. 2 depicts the proportions of the various topics in the corpus. The most frequently occurring challenges were Executive Presence and Change Management. The least frequent challenges were Leading Teams and Leading Others. As a point of comparison between the most common and least common challenges, Executive Presence was identified as a challenge about twice as often as Leading Teams.

Relationship between topics. Next, we examined the relationship between the topics. In general, because topic prevalence across all topics must add to 100%, we would anticipate that the prevalence of most topics would be negatively correlated with one another. This would be especially true if the documents tended to be dominated by a single topic. As the prevalence for the dominant topic increases, the prevalence for other topics would necessarily decrease. This is the exact pattern that we observed with the leader challenge documents (see Appendix A). However, even if there was a single dominant challenge described in many documents, we are still interested in knowing if other challenges are being discussed simultaneously. To identify these other less-dominant, yet co-occurring challenges, we examined the observed topic prevalence and dichotomously coded those topics as either above or below the median topic prevalence and correlated the result. Fig. 3 contains a graphical depiction of those relationships. The ties between topics represent correlations among topics greater than or equal to 0.16, and the size of the nodes indicate topic prevalence. We chose 0.16 based on Bosco and colleagues (2015) correlational benchmarks that found the median field level effect size in the applied psychology literature to be 0.16 (Bosco et al., 2015). This visualization indicates that there is a relationship between reporting Executive Presence as a challenge and reporting challenges related to Communication. As another example, challenges related to Daily Management Activities, Business Unit Issues, and Role Transition were all positively related meaning that all three challenges were more likely to appear in the same document. To further illustrate the rela-1 Two PhD doctoral students, blind to fact that nine topics was identified as the optimal quantitative solution, were asked to independently evaluate the qualitative coherence of the nine-topic solution along with the eight-and ten-topic solutions, which were identified as the two next best fitting solutions based on the quantitative metrics, using the top word metrics described below. Both subject matter experts indicated that the nine-topic solution appeared to be more coherent and felt like it would be easier to generate meaningful labels for the nine topic solution compared to the eight-and ten-topic solutions. More details about evaluating topic quality both quantitatively and qualitatively are presented in the manuscript. Since both quantitative metrics and subjective evaluations of alternative solutions converged on nine topics, for the remainder of the manuscript, we focus on the nine-topic solution. Interested readers can review the eight-and ten-topic solutions at (https://osf.io/us9pb/?view_only=e7a6238dba8b46969ba4b7becf85ec7d).

tionship between topics, Table 5 contains exemplar documents that scored high on both Communication and Executive Presence.

Relationship between topic prevalence and meta-data. Next, we examined the relationship between challenge prevalence and document meta-data. This meta-data included information about a leader's background and characteristics as well as the leader's performance as rated by their boss. To analyze these relationships, we fit an estimation model to the data based on the following generic model:

Where i indexes the ith participant and j indexes the jth topic. Prevalence ij is the matrix of topic prevalence for each participant's challenge document obtained from conducting the topic model analysis. Gender, Race, OrgLevel, and U.S. national are categorical variables. Gender and U.S. national were dichotomous variables with females and non-U.S. nationals serving as the reference category, respectively. Race and organizational level were multi-categorical variables that were dummy coded. Those additional terms were omitted from the equation above for simplicity. The reference categories for those variables were Caucasian and middle management. Finally, performance was a continuous variable. Results from these analyses are contained in Table 6. Because these analyses were performed across all nine challenges, we elected to be slightly more conservative and focus our interpretation on only those relationships where p < .01.

Fig. 4 contains a graphical depiction of the significant differences as a function of gender. Females were significantly more likely to report challenges related to Executive Presence, Communication, and Leading Others compared to males. In contrast, males were more likely to remark about challenges related to Daily Management Activities, Business Unit Issues and Change Management. Given the large sample sizes, one might be wary of interpreting statistical significance. However, these differences are still meaningful when expressed in terms of common language measures of effect size. As context for under-standing these differences, one can examine the magnitude of a specific gender effect in relation to the overall topic prevalence. Doing so reveals that females were 24% more likely to report a challenge related to Executive Presence, 19% more likely to report a challenge related to Leading Others, and 12% more likely to report a challenge related to Communication compared to males, and males were 50%, 43%, and 14% more likely than females to report challenges related to Daily Management Activities, Business Unit Issues, and Change Management, respectively.

Fig. 5 contains a graphical depiction of the significant differences as a function of race where we compare Caucasians to African Americans. The larger confidence intervals in this figure compared to Fig. 4 are a result of the disproportionately fewer number of African American leaders in the sample. There were two significant differences in the prevalence of challenges between these two groups. African Americans reported more challenges related to Prioritizing Work and Nonwork Responsibilities (32% more likely) and fewer challenges related to Daily Management Activities (35% less likely) when compared to Caucasians. There were no statistically significant differences between Caucasians and Hispanics, so those results are not illustrated.

We also examined whether participants at different organizational levels were more or less likely to report certain challenges. An inspection of Table 7 reveals that executives were 38% more likely to report Role Transition as a challenge compared to mid-level managers. When contrasting mid-level managers with front-line supervisors, mid-level managers were significantly more likely to report Role Transition (25%), Business Unit Issues (75%), and Change Management (17%) as a meaningful challenge, whereas front-line supervisors were more likely to report Prioritizing Work and Nonwork Responsibilities (22%), Daily Management Responsibilities (19%), and Executive Presence as challenges (16%). Fig. 6 depicts the differences between executives and front-line managers. Executives reported significantly more challenges related to Role Transition and Business Unit Issues whereas front-line supervisors reported more challenges related to Prioritizing Work and Nonwork Responsibilities, Leading Others, and Executive Presence. Fig. 7 contains a depiction of the differences in challenges reported by U.S. nationals versus non-U.S. nationals. U.S. nationals were 25% more likely to report challenges related to Role Transition and 28% more likely to report challenges related to Daily Management Activities, whereas non-U.S. nationals were 9%, 37%, and 44% more likely to report challenges related to Communication, Leading Teams, and Business Unit Issues respectively.

Finally, we examined the relationship between topic prevalence and boss' ratings of performance. Two challenges were significantly related to boss' performance ratings: Daily Management Activities and Communication. There was a positive relationship between performance ratings and the prevalence of reporting Daily Management Activities as a significant challenge (Fig. 8). In contrast, there was a significant negative relationship between performance ratings and the prevalence of reporting Communication as a significant challenge (Fig. 9).

Relationship between topic content and meta-data. In addition to the above analyses that relate topic prevalence to meta-data, the metadata can also be used as content covariates. What this means is one is able to investigate whether the words that comprise the various topics, the content of the topics, differs as a function of these other variables. We illustrate this process by examining the impact of respondent gender and race on two topics, Communication and Daily Management Activities. Previously, we discovered that the prevalence for the topic Communication differed between men and women with women more frequently identifying this as a challenge. But, when men and women discuss this challenge of Communication, are they talking about it in the same way? Using STM, we can easily surface the dominant words being used by each group when discussing this topic. Fig. 10 contains a graphical depiction of the result. Interestingly, when males cite communication as a challenge, they tend to talk about it in terms of developing, improving, or getting better at their communication skills. In contrast, one of the dominant words when females cite Communication as a challenge is confidence. As another example, we examine how African Americans and Caucasians talk about challenges related to Daily Management Activities. In this instance, there wasn't a significant difference in the prevalence of this challenge between African Americans and Caucasians. Yet, both groups talk about these two challenges in very different ways (see Fig. 11). When Caucasians discuss this challenge, their Daily Management issues seem to center on managing employees. In contrast, African Americans who face this challenge tend to talk about resources as underlying this challenge. sections.paper_id: Tonidandel et al. - 2022 - Using structural topic modeling to gain insight into challenges faced by leaders
paragraphs: The call for papers for this special issue specifically asks how insights from computer algorithms can be used to better understand leadership. Our paper directly addresses this question by illustrating the power of using STM as a tool for inductively generating a classification of leader challenges. We identified nine central challenges leaders face in this sample. Several of the topics we identified converged with those from smaller scale qualitative studies, which is an encouraging sign for leader development research and practice particularly when such replication is encouraged with a strong methodology and power (Antonakis, 2017). For example, the challenges from the Gentry et al. (2014) study of leaders attending a program targeted for middle-to-senior-level managers (Developing Employees, Leading a Team, and Guiding Change) mirror the topics we identified of Leading Others, Leading Teams, and Change Management in this study of leaders attending a program targeted primarily for new leaders and frontline, first-level, or mid-level managers or supervisors. Notwithstanding these parallels, we were also able to identify new challenges not previously identified in those samples such as Role Transition. With the size of our sample and its diversity, we were also able to explore relationships between leader characteristics and the challenges that have been largely unexplored and correlate those challenges with outcomes, thereby advancing theoretical understanding of challenges within a nomological network. We discuss our theoretical and practical contributions below.

sections.paper_id: Tonidandel et al. - 2022 - Using structural topic modeling to gain insight into challenges faced by leaders
paragraphs: Tonidandel et al. ( 2018) appeal to researchers to leverage big data sources and methods to help build a greater understanding of the organizational sciences. Our illustration of STM on leader challenges is in response to their call and helps advance our understanding of leader development through the analysis of a large corpus of unstructured text and the application of machine learning. Big data is often characterized by several V's: volume, variety, and velocity. Concerning volume, we began with a large corpus of approximately 8000 unstructured text documents containing over 300,000 tokens (columns in the data set). Though not big data in a technical sense, this large amount of text could not have been analyzed using traditional human coded content analysis methods allowing us to illustrate the methods that are suitable for large volumes of text typically found in big data projects. Variety was also an element of our data as we tied these unstructured text responses to leaders' demographic information and performance data from their bosses. Finally, illustrating the velocity element of big data, the resulting algorithmic topic classification can

Table 4 Exemplars of the highest probability documents in each topic Topic 1: Role Transition New Director of a division / recent promotion-I've been with the organization for 10 years but I have been recently promotoed to Division Director level and also moved from NYC office to DC office. The main challenge is adapting to new city, new environment, new office culture, etc.. Brand new to the position. Uncertainty about new role. Trying to earn the respect from the group regarding new role. Company was bought a year ago. Trying to impress the new senior management and educate them about the work we do at my office. Having my boss treat me like the outright leader of the group. Topic 2: Prioritizing Work and Nonwork Responsibilities Balancing work life and family life -time management. Organizing my life in order to earn my associate's degree. Prioritizing my life in order to earn my bachelor's degree. Time management. Balancing time spent doing tangible work related tasks and time spent in meetings and dealings with co workers or subordinates Topic 3: Daily Management Activities Keeping up with the environmental health and safety training requirements for our employees. This is an ever changing/evolving process. Constantly monitor policy changes. Keeping the permitting current for continued facility operation. Maintaining facility mobile equipment equipment The firefighting (metaphorical) keeps me too busy to do much long term stratigization. High turnover rates have cause us to loose much of our long term knowledge base. Poor plant performance has led to significant moral issues within the plant population Topic 4: Leading Others I recently transitioned into a supervisory role and my direct report were previously my peers. Gaining the respect and the confidence from my group as their supervisor versus their peer is more difficult than I originally imagined. Good working relationship with my direct report. Trust between myself and my direct report. Feedback / feed forward. Ensuring that our internal systems are up to date to help keep us in compliance. I'm sometimes very agressive to subordinates when something is not done right or when something goes wrong, and end up saying some things which end up haunting me afterwards. Which sometimes makes subordinates shy away in asking for assistance or make them scared. I take my work too personally. I let my emotions get involved, and carry my work home with me in the mental and emotional sense. Because of how personally I take my job and my work, I tend to become a control freak when something involves my particular territory. Influence in general but particularly other executives without being an executive. Dealing with passive/aggressive resistance to change Leading and managing change effectively. Dealing with uncertainty. Motivating employees Facilitating open communication. Topic 9:
Leading Teams Motivation for both team member and myself. Team is very stressed out over work, fun is not there. Feel that team doesn't trust me to represent them at leaders table.
Keep the team motivated. Keep the team unity. Handle individual follow up.
Note. Edited for brevity be applied almost instantaneously to new data such that leader challenges can be easily discoverable in newly generated text.

In addition to using a large corpus of unstructured text data, this paper also incorporates modern data science methods in our study of leader challenges. As previously noted, qualitative methods do not easily scale to such a large amount of text data. Moreover, traditional tools like CATA require a predefined dictionary and fail to account for word context. In contrast, NLP methods such as STM enable researchers to inductively build an understanding of massive amounts of text data and to impose a meaningful structure on that data to further our awareness of leader challenges. Although human coders are the sine qua non of textual evaluation and understanding, STMs may confer some advantages over a human coder because this methodology affords the opportunity to incorporate document meta-data into the analysis of every document. STM solutions may be slightly more nuanced than one produced by a human coder who cannot simultaneously attend to all of those additional features of the text document when doing the coding. STMs are also a clear methodological advancement over simple word count meth-ods in that word co-occurrences are used to infer similar semantic meaning in STM. Nevertheless, future researchers should explore integrating other advances in NLP such as word vectorization into STMs to further leverage the algorithm's ability to infer the semantic meaning of words and phrases.

Contributions to leader development. Leader training and development should not be a generic, one-size-fits-all program, event, or initiative (Gentry et al., 2014;Lacerenza et al., 2017). Our research, based on data from thousands of managers, suggests what content leaders expect to be covered in a program targeted to new leaders or frontline, first-level, or mid-level managers or supervisors. These findings should serve to enhance learning and transfer because any local needs assessment may now rely on this evidence to inform their own training program and delivery. In what follows, we examine what should be covered in leader development initiatives based on the results of our STM analysis of leader challenges and briefly discuss why specific challenges may be related to performance outcomes.

Our data infer that individual leader characteristics (e.g., gender, race, citizen status, and organizational level) may influence the types of challenges individuals report facing. Responses to questions about key leadership challenges may reflect fundamental differences in how different demographic groups experience leadership rather than a specific objective reality that leaders are facing. When leaders are asked to list their key leadership challenges as a part of a leader development program, it is within a context where individuals are given an opportunity to reflect on the key leadership areas they want to focus on and develop.

When examining the gender differences found in our results, one should consider the seminal research by Belenky et al. (1986). They found women pursue knowledge through connection. Therefore, women's reported challenges in the present data around Communication, Executive Presence, and Leading Others in the current research aligns well with how they approach and understand learning. On the other hand, men use a more action-focused, task-oriented approach to learning (Belenky et al., 1986). Arguably, Daily Management Activ-ities, Business Unit Issues, and Change Management are concrete, action-centered contexts where men may be most comfortable talking about and focusing their efforts to improve their leadership skills. Similar logic is reflected in how men and women talk about challenges with Communication, for example. Interestingly, when men cite Communication as a challenge, they tend to talk about it in terms of developing, improving, or getting better at their communication skills. In contrast, one of the dominant words when women cite Communication as a challenge is confidence.

Another possible perspective of these differences may be found in the differential feedback men and women receive and hence, what they then may process as current challenges. Doldor et al. (2019) show that women and men receive "gendered" feedback, meaning the feedback men and women receive varies in the extent to which their leadership potential is acknowledged during the feedback process, and what specific advice is offered to them in terms of how they should Table 5 Exemplars of documents scoring high on both Communication and Executive Presence I would like to improve my influencing and negotiation skills by gaining the cooperation of others to obtain information and accomplish goals. This will allow me to create a win/win scenario for all parties involved. I would like to expand my exposure to the political aspects (both external and internal). This will allow me to build a clear perception of organizational and political reality by recognizing the impact of all courses of action. I would like to establish visionary skills by assessing long-term goals and be able to serve as a catalyst for agency change. I understand I will need to learn how to best build a shared vision and how to put that vision into action. Presenting a confident image, influence others, oral Communication skills. Much of the work conducted here is by phone conference and often includes people all over the country or different countries. In addition, communication is commonly across very different functions and often with people you have not met or worked with. My challenge in these situations is to come across on the phone with confidence and expertise and to contribute to the joint effort. Each partner has a unique mission and set of goals and objectives. Developing a clear vision and clearly commutating this with partners is the most critical leadership challenge I currently face. Drafting clear goals and strategies that result in tangible accomplishments that engage partners is critical to these activities. Personalities are diverse and goals sometimes are in conflict the ability to find common ground is critical. I have great staff, and need to do a better job of showing my appreciation. I recognize that sometimes I do not provide them with measureable objectives that translate into meaningful benchmarks. I also need to do a better job of thanking or rewarding them for completing their assignments. I should also do a better job of letting them know how important their contribution is to the success of the partnership. When involved in tense situations that require significant compromise it is difficult for me to develop alternatives that provide win-win situations. I would like to develop the leadership skills necessary to develop innovative solutions when polarized sides are at an impasse. Our organization is going through an unprecedented change strategy. All levels of managment are struggling with the change. I need to find a way to influence upwards to help move our organization forward on this change path. I want to be able to form and lead highly functional teams to help the organization transition to the next phase in our change process I feel I am very creative, have great ideas and know I have some of the solutions in how to get our organization on the right track and to motivate the employees to embrace the change comeing and move through it, but I don't feel I am being heard. I don't think I communicate my ideas well enough that they resonate with our executive management team. I need to find a way to be heard. I'm having a hard time communicating with my peer group of C-level executives. My ideas are often counter to theirs, but often eventually implemented and seen as the way to move forward however my communication is probably poor around these issues. Controlling my emotions is difficult because I feel of sense of urgency around these issues and am not satisfied with the rate of change within the organization. How do I know if I'm being an effective leader in the organization if some people love working with me while others detest it. Note. a Organizational level was coded as front-line, middle, executive, or not applicable) with middle management as the reference category. b Race was coded as Caucasian, African American, Hispanic, Other, or not reported with Caucasian as the reference category. Gender and US national were dichotomous with females and US nationals as the reference categories, respectively. *p < .05, **p < .01, ***p < .001 perform as leaders, as well as how to develop and advance as leaders.

For example, women were advised to develop interpersonal sensitivity (e.g., have confidence, get along with others) while men were advised to step into more leadership positions and actively pursue their leadership aspirations (e.g., project or display confidence). In our sample, the challenges women reported more often than men mirror this phe-nomenon: women were more likely to report a challenge related to Executive Presence, Leading Others, and Communication (aligning with interpersonal behaviors) compared to men who were more likely to report challenges related to Daily Management Activities, Business Unit Issues, and Change Management (aligning with task-related performance).

Fig. 4. Difference in challenge prevalence by gender. Note. Dots represent point estimates for the difference between challenge prevalence for the two groups. The lines bracketing the dots represent the 99% Confidence Interval (CI) of that difference. A CI that includes 0.00 is not statistically significant.

Fig. 5. Difference in challenge prevalence by race comparing Caucasians and African Americans. Note. Dots represent point estimates for the difference between challenge prevalence for the two groups. The lines bracketing the dots represent the 99% Confidence Interval (CI) of that difference. A CI that includes 0.00 is not statistically significant.

The differences in reported challenges among ethnic groups (i.e., Caucasian vs. African American) and across cultures (i.e., U.S. vs. non-US) may also reflect both fundamental differences in understanding leadership and real-world realities. For instance, Chin (2013) examined within-country differences on GLOBE dimensions for leaders across five racial/ethnic groups in the U.S. (White, Black, Hispanic, Asian, and Native American Indians) and found important variations across race and ethnicity in their perceptions of leadership. Chin (2013) attributed these differences to the common lived experience of minority status in the U.S. and of marginality and oppression among diverse leaders of color that set them apart from the White males in their study. Though leaders across all five racial/ethnic groups endorsed leadership dimensions consistent with the "universal" dimensions found in the GLOBE studies, they were higher on Humane, Self-Protective, and Participative leadership dimensions and lower on the Charismatic dimension compared to the GLOBE Anglo profile. Perhaps African Americans reported fewer challenges related to Daily Management Activities because they want to protect themselves from being overrun by Daily Management Activities. They may view the Prioritizing of Work and Non-Work Responsibilities as the more fruitful areas to focus on long term as a strategy for reducing the extent to which Daily Management Activities overtake their family and personal lives. Future research is needed to test these assertions, however.

As a result, women leaders may have to address unique stereotypes known to affect perceptions of leader performance and effectiveness, which may serve as an obstacle to gaining legitimacy, or Executive Presence as a leader. Context has become a substantial area of interest within leadership studies (see Burak, 2018 for a review). Therefore, it is notable that differences were found in challenges reported based on citizen status. Such diversity in team composition increases the need for effective Communication and Team Leadership practices for positive work outcomes. Business unit issues are often regulatory and local market issues that may also be newer for non-US nationals to learn and adapt to.

Finally, we found differences according to organizational level. Leaders at higher organizational levels have different challenges than leaders at lower organizational levels. Role Transition was more prevalent at higher organizational levels compared to lower levels; Prioritizing Work and Nonwork Responsibilities, Daily Management Responsibilities, and Executive Presence were more prevalent at lower organizational levels than higher levels. As stratified systems theory (Jaques, 1978) asserts, leadership requirements differ across organizational levels. Leaders at lower levels need to understand, formulate, and execute on the work while those at higher levels have to deal with systems, complexities, and ambiguities. Indeed, front-line supervisors and those at lower levels of the organization likely have less discretion

In addition to using a large corpus of unstructured text data, this paper also incorporates modern data science methods in our study of leader challenges. As previously noted, qualitative methods do not easily scale to such a large amount of text data. Moreover, traditional tools like CATA require a predefined dictionary and fail to account for word context. In contrast, NLP methods such as STM enable researchers to inductively build an understanding of massive amounts of text data and to impose a meaningful structure on that data to further our awareness of leader challenges. Although human coders are the sine qua non of textual evaluation and understanding, STMs may confer some advantages over a human coder because this methodology affords the opportunity to incorporate document meta-data into the analysis of every document. STM solutions may be slightly more nuanced than one produced by a human coder who cannot simultaneously attend to all of those additional features of the text document when doing the coding. STMs are also a clear methodological advancement over simple word count meth-ods in that word co-occurrences are used to infer similar semantic meaning in STM. Nevertheless, future researchers should explore integrating other advances in NLP such as word vectorization into STMs to further leverage the algorithm's ability to infer the semantic meaning of words and phrases.

Contributions to leader development. Leader training and development should not be a generic, one-size-fits-all program, event, or initiative (Gentry et al., 2014;Lacerenza et al., 2017).
