These findings should serve to enhance learning and transfer because any local needs assessment may now rely on this evidence to inform their own training program and delivery. In what follows, we examine what should be covered in leader development initiatives based on the results of our STM analysis of leader challenges and briefly discuss why specific challenges may be related to performance outcomes.

Our data infer that individual leader characteristics (e.g., gender, race, citizen status, and organizational level) may influence the types of challenges individuals report facing. Responses to questions about key leadership challenges may reflect fundamental differences in how different demographic groups experience leadership rather than a specific objective reality that leaders are facing. When leaders are asked to list their key leadership challenges as a part of a leader development program, it is within a context where individuals are given an opportunity to reflect on the key leadership areas they want to focus on and develop.

When examining the gender differences found in our results, one should consider the seminal research by Belenky et al. (1986). They found women pursue knowledge through connection. Therefore, women's reported challenges in the present data around Communication, Executive Presence, and Leading Others in the current research aligns well with how they approach and understand learning. On the other hand, men use a more action-focused, task-oriented approach to learning (Belenky et al., 1986). Arguably, Daily Management Activ-ities, Business Unit Issues, and Change Management are concrete, action-centered contexts where men may be most comfortable talking about and focusing their efforts to improve their leadership skills. Interestingly, when men cite Communication as a challenge, they tend to talk about it in terms of developing, improving, or getting better at their communication skills. In contrast, one of the dominant words when women cite Communication as a challenge is confidence.

Though leaders across all five racial/ethnic groups endorsed leadership dimensions consistent with the "universal" dimensions found in the GLOBE studies, they were higher on Humane, Self-Protective, and Participative leadership dimensions and lower on the Charismatic dimension compared to the GLOBE Anglo profile. Perhaps African Americans reported fewer challenges related to Daily Management Activities because they want to protect themselves from being overrun by Daily Management Activities. They may view the Prioritizing of Work and Non-Work Responsibilities as the more fruitful areas to focus on long term as a strategy for reducing the extent to which Daily Management Activities overtake their family and personal lives. Future research is needed to test these assertions, however.

Another way to look at the differences regarding gender and race is through a social identity perspective. Gender and race identities are both non-prototypical leadership identities. As a result, leaders who possess minority identities may be more likely to engage in more identity work in leader development contexts, meaning they internalize their leader identity through a set of relational and social processes through which they come to see themselves, and are seen by others, as a leader (Hogg, 2001;DeRue & Ashford, 2010). Gender leadership research based on the lack-of-fit model (Heilman, 1983(Heilman, , 2001)), role congruity theory (Eagly & Karau, 2002), and the status incongruity hypothesis offer parallel accounts of agentic women who face social penalties for engaging in counter stereotypical norms (Rosette et al., 2018). As a result, women leaders may have to address unique stereotypes known to affect perceptions of leader performance and effectiveness, which may serve as an obstacle to gaining legitimacy, or Executive Presence as a leader. Women in leadership are likely highly aware of these stereotypes and the challenges they pose for their leadership effectiveness may be particularly salient.

Context has become a substantial area of interest within leadership studies (see Burak, 2018 for a review). Therefore, it is notable that differences were found in challenges reported based on citizen status. More specifically, U.S. nationals reported significantly more challenges related to Role Transition and Daily Management Activities, whereas non-U.S. nationals were more likely to report challenges related to Communication and Leading Teams, and Business Unit Issues. Given that non-U.S. nationals were attending a leader development program in the U.S., it is likely that these individuals were leading teams composed of individuals from a variety of cultural backgrounds. Such diversity in team composition increases the need for effective Communication and Team Leadership practices for positive work outcomes. Business unit issues are often regulatory and local market issues that may also be newer for non-US nationals to learn and adapt to.

Finally, we found differences according to organizational level. Leaders at higher organizational levels have different challenges than leaders at lower organizational levels. Role Transition was more prevalent at higher organizational levels compared to lower levels; Prioritizing Work and Nonwork Responsibilities, Daily Management Responsibilities, and Executive Presence were more prevalent at lower organizational levels than higher levels. As stratified systems theory (Jaques, 1978) asserts, leadership requirements differ across organizational levels. Leaders at lower levels need to understand, formulate, and execute on the work while those at higher levels have to deal with systems, complexities, and ambiguities. Indeed, front-line supervisors and those at lower levels of the organization likely have less discretion

Our illustration of STM on leader challenges is in response to their call and helps advance our understanding of leader development through the analysis of a large corpus of unstructured text and the application of machine learning. Big data is often characterized by several V's: volume, variety, and velocity.

sections.header_raw: The relationship of challenge type and performance
sections.header_norm: the relationship of challenge type and performance
paragraphs: Our research also provides insight into how reported challenges may relate to leader performance. Through STM, we uncovered a correlation between certain challenges and boss-rated performance: a significant positive relationship between performance ratings and the prevalence of reporting Daily Management Activities as well as a significant negative relationship between performance ratings and the prevalence of reporting Communication. Daily Management Activities involves the core "task-related" work of leadership which is about getting results, and such work is an inherent part of effective leaders (Judge et al., 2004). Many leaders are promoted into formal management positions based on this (Benson et al., 2019;Gentry, 2016) and perhaps remains an implicit belief of what makes a good leader. When individuals discuss this key challenge with their bosses as part of the leader development program, it may reflect well on the individual that they are so focused on "getting results that matter". On the other hand, communication is an essential skill for anyone in the professional Fig. 6. Difference in challenge prevalence by organizational level comparing executives and front-line supervisors. Note. Dots represent point estimates for the difference between challenge prevalence for the two groups. The lines bracketing the dots represent the 99% Confidence Interval (CI) of that difference. A CI that includes 0.00 is not statistically significant. The lines bracketing the dots represent the 99% Confidence Interval (CI) of that difference. A CI that includes 0.00 is not statistically significant.

workforce (NACE, 2017). If one does not communicate well, it may be difficult to perform in any job, and thus, leaders may simply be expected to be effective at communication and thus, get penalized for not having good communication skills. Therefore, when individuals discuss this as a key challenge (real or perceived) with their bosses, it may signal that the individual does not have the potential to effectively lead in their current role.

sections.paper_id: Tonidandel et al. - 2022 - Using structural topic modeling to gain insight into challenges faced by leaders
paragraphs: A meta-analysis suggested some leadership development programs are "tremendously effective" while others "failed miserably" (Collins & Holton, 2004, 232). A more recent meta-analysis found that leadership training can be highly effective if certain design, delivery, and implementation features are taken into account (Lacerenza et al.,Fig. 8. Relationship between boss' ratings of performance and reporting Daily Management Activities as a significant challenge. 2017). Ideally, for any training to be most effective, it should target the most important competencies. This is why a needs assessment is an integral part of any training design process (Gentry et al., 2014;Lacerenza et al., 2017). Past research (Baldwin & Ford, 1988;Blume et al., 2010) suggests such needs assessments may help fill the aforementioned gaps in leader development mentioned by Moldoveanu and Narayandas (2019) Therefore, practitioners of leadership development programs may come to understand the value of STM and the findings on leader challenges specifically from this study as a starting point for helping shape the content they teach in their programs for a similar leader population. Whenever possible, a local training needs assessment (see Goldstein & Ford, 2002) should also take place within an organization to determine the specific type and prevalence of each challenge as well as how other key stakeholders (e.g., direct reports, bosses, coaches) perceive the leaders' challenges. Then, practitioners can more precisely link the key leader skills and attributes (Mumford et al., 2007;Zaccaro et al., 2013) to the most critical and prevalent challenges in their organizational context. By taking the time to anchor skills and attributes to key leadership challenges, practitioners will be more likely to design and deliver training content that meets the needs of both individuals and organizations (Moldoveanu & Narayandas, 2019).

Though these data come from only the leader's perspective about the challenges faced on-the-job, they also represent a key (but often neglected) input that should increase the quality of a leader competency model. Competency models refer to collections of knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics (KSAOs) that are needed for effective performance in the jobs in question. According to the seminal review of best practices in competency modeling (Campion et al., 2011), the process often starts with a definition of the organizational goals and objectives. Then, the competencies critical for obtaining those goals and objectives are identified. To identify the competencies, input is often first collected from top executives and then usually from leaders at all levels as well as from different perspectives (e.g., subordinate, superiors, customers). This input is collected via focus groups, interviews, and surveys. Data may often be collected around the contextual factors that may impact improvement on competencies and business strategy achievement goals (e.g., organizational culture, life stage, market, customers, employee relations, presence of a union, and strengths and weaknesses of its management) and the importance of various skills right now versus in the future. Though organizations may collect data on leadership challenges in focus groups and interviews, it is not clear from Campion et al. (2011) if this is the case or how often it happens. In recent years, many consultants (e.g., Bernal & Schuller, 2016) are criticizing the entire competency modeling industry. They say that most competency models do not take into account the leader's context or that they are just too long and complex to be useful. These critiques suggest that most organizations may not systematically collect data on leadership challenges at scale as a means to prioritize the competencies that matter most. Though there are many possible reasons for why practitioners develop laundry lists of competencies (e.g., time pressure or lack of in-house data analytic skills to create parsimonious models), we believe that the industry's ability to quickly and efficiently deploy STM on large scale challenge data sets, generated by the simple prompt we have used in this study, will increase the likelihood that organizations will investigate leader challenges as a key input in the competency modeling process. Deploying an STM algorithm on open-ended text can make the data gathering and analysis process less painful, less time consuming, and offers useful insights about what all leaders (and from the perspective of others such as bosses, peers, etc.) in an organization are facing instead of just a select few who get to participate in interviews and focus groups. In doing so, we believe that learning transfer will improve because the identified skills for development from the competency modeling process will be more likely to address the leaders' actual on-the-job challenges.

Additionally, our findings also offer training practitioners insights into possible strategies for developing more relevant and inclusive programs for different leader populations. Though future research is needed to better understand why certain demographic groups report specific types of challenges before more specific interventions can take place, it may be useful to introduce these findings to trainees as a way to improve awareness of the various ways that various demographic groups make sense of leadership and the real or perceived challenges they face. Recent research suggests that current models of leadership and leadership training do not incorporate the perspectives and worldviews of diverse individuals (Chin, 2013;Chin et al., 2016). The insights around contextual factors, such as citizen status and organizational level, suggest that training practitioners may want to offer more specific training opportunities for individuals operating across different contexts. For example, extra training time and coaching around Business Unit issues may be provided to leaders at lower levels who identify as non-U.S. nationals operating in U.S. and training programs could be more focused on equipping them with skills and attributes related to Prioritizing Work and Nonwork Responsibilities, Daily Management Responsibilities, and Executive Presence; whereas programs for more senior leaders should be focused on training skills and attributes related to Role Transition, Business Unit Issues, and Change Management.

Organizational scholars have long employed text analysis techniques to understand leadership phenomenon (Insch et al., 1997). As in many fields, the availability of textual data has heightened the appeal of textual analysis for investigating leadership constructs and processes. A broader review of applications of text-based analysis reveals that common data sources used to perform these methods include annual reports, trade magazines, notes from interviews, other internal company documents and open-ended questions in surveys. Mission statements, business cases, and transcribed video tapes are a few, less commonly used resources (Duriau et al., 2007).

The different legal and ethical considerations that come with using particular forms of text data form a final limitation. Some text data are proprietary or contain privacy sensitive information that may be difficult to anonymize. However, pattern matching and machine learning methods can be used to de-identify data (Meystre et al., 2014). The difficulty of obtaining permission to use text data can be addressed in part by implementing safeguards to protect the confidentiality of the data and to perform analyses securely. Wider ethical concerns (Van Wel & Royakkers, 2004) on the use of "big" data, urgently need further and wider development and discussion.

sections.paper_id: Tonidandel et al. - 2022 - Using structural topic modeling to gain insight into challenges faced by leaders
paragraphs: As this study is the first to apply STM to a large corpus of leader responses to generate a classification of leader challenges, there are a number of limitations that must be acknowledged. We applied this approach to participants' responses in a single leadership development program targeted for new leaders and managers and supervisors who are at frontline, first-level, or mid-level positions in organizations. Although 8000 leaders is a sizable sample, these participants may not present the full universe of challenges experienced by all leaders.

Our global sample is a positive aspect of the study. Yet, we also acknowledge that the race variable may be a limitation, as the choices could appear to be U.S.-centric and not account for the diversity around the world (e.g., "African American" may not be applicable to many outside of the U.S.). Specifically, 2611 of the 3229 people not from the U.S. did not report their race or reported race as "other". A small number of people outside the U.S. did report their race and were used in the analyses. However, the analyses are regression based so terms being evaluated are "controlling" for other terms in the model. As a result, when examining race differences (for example, when we compare African Americans to Caucasians) results compare differences when nationality is held constant (i.e., the sample is 90% from the U.S. and nationality is being "controlled" for in the analysis).

In addition, our sample is not a random sample of leaders as participants and their employers elected to participate in this specific leadership development program. As a result, the potential exists that the challenges reported might differ from those collected from a random sample of leaders. We should continue to apply this methodology to other samples of leaders, especially at higher leader levels. Other more advanced approaches that rely on deep learning techniques like GLoVe (Pennington et al., 2014), BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) may be worth exploring. We applied this approach to participants' responses in a single leadership development program targeted for new leaders and managers and supervisors who are at frontline, first-level, or mid-level positions in organizations. Although 8000 leaders is a sizable sample, these participants may not present the full universe of challenges experienced by all leaders.

Our global sample is a positive aspect of the study. Yet, we also acknowledge that the race variable may be a limitation, as the choices could appear to be U.S.-centric and not account for the diversity around the world (e.g., "African American" may not be applicable to many outside of the U.S.). Specifically, 2611 of the 3229 people not from the U.S. did not report their race or reported race as "other". A small number of people outside the U.S. did report their race and were used in the analyses. However, the analyses are regression based so terms being evaluated are "controlling" for other terms in the model. sections.paper_id: Tonidandel et al. - 2022 - Using structural topic modeling to gain insight into challenges faced by leaders
sections.header_raw: Applying topic models to leadership research
sections.header_norm: applying topic models to leadership research
paragraphs: As previously noted, Hannigan et al. (2019) describe five primary ways topic modeling can advance management research. We illustrate one of these ways in this paper, developing an inductive classification system, and apply it to leader challenges. These same methods can be profitably applied to other areas of leadership research in need of classification. For example, one application is to classify field level insights by rendering the topics within a field or journal. Antons et al. (2016) enacted such an approach applied to the Journal of Product Innovation Management. A similar approach could be applied to The Leadership Quarterly to induce categories of topics being studied, examine topic trends over time, and so on.

A second purpose of topic modeling is to detect novelty and emergence. Topic models in general permit scientists to inductively generate topics from a body of text without making prior assumptions about the findings (Roberts et al., 2014). Therefore, topic models can help to discover what new constructs may be relevant or how language related to existing constructs changes and evolves. Given that topic models are applied to a fundamental and learned form of human communication used to express meaningwritten texttopic modeling also provides a window into human cognition. Thus, topic models can also be applied to better understand managerial cognition and behavior. For example, Wilson and Joseph (2015) apply topic modeling to patent documents to show that the attentional structure (i.e., organizational attention given to problems within and across business units; a cognitive mechanism) affects an organization's ability to broaden their information search scope and depth. In a similar way, leadership researchers can analyze managers' language use in written responses to open-ended survey responses to gain insight into how leaders themselves think about various leadership activities such as information searching, communication, and decision-making. Such data might be contrasted with the language use of followers to identify similarities and differences between leader and followers' perceptions of leader behavior to develop a more fine-grained understanding of relationship dynamics between leaders and followers.

Topic models can also be used to understand how online audiences evaluate companies and products (Hannigan et al., 2019). A related application in the leadership domain would be to use topic modeling to understand how followers perceive and evaluate leaders. Corpora may be derived from qualitative responses in 360 feedback data or from sites like glassdoor.com (Schmiedel et al. 2019). Applying topic modeling to these unstructured texts can identify what behaviors followers focus on when describing the strengths and weaknesses of a leader and capture the more subtle aspects being attended to by evaluators. Additionally, metadata about both the leader and follower could be included in such an analysis to see how the topics and related structures differ based on the characteristics of the two parties. For example, do the topics discussed in textual evaluations of leaders differ based on the gender of the leaders, the gender of the follower, or an interaction between the two? Topic modeling can also be used to analyze frames and social movements to answer the question, "What is going on here?" (Hannigan et al., 2019). One way leadership scholars may utilize topic modeling is to better understand the critical incidents that lead to organizational success or failure is by rendering topics from CEO communications during meetings leading up to the end of a profitable quarter versus a less profitable quarter. Another interesting research direction might be to investigate the different ways CEOs frame diversity before and after the rise of social movements such as Black Lives Matter or Me Too movements and how these frames relate to the organization's ability to recruit and retain diverse talent, develop successful and diverse work teams, and appeal to consumers from various identity backgrounds. Several leadership studies using cross-sectional survey data reveal women and men value leader styles and behaviors (e.g., likeability, agency, charisma) differently (Eagly & Johannesen-Schmidt, 2001;Geys, 2014;Livingston et al., 2012). STM may offer a more nuanced look at the competing frames associated with leader identity, styles, and behavior as they relate to male and female leaders and further, how these frames covary as a function of the evaluators' characteristics (e.g., gender, race, cultural background, etc.).

A final application of topic models applied to leadership is to help understand cultural dynamics. Management scholars often rely on survey data to classify individuals along cultural value dimensions (e.g., individualism-collectivism, power distance, etc.). STMs can be used to inductively derive cultural concepts and meanings and investigate how they evolve over time rather than making a priori assumptions about cultural beliefs. In the context of leadership, big data can be leveraged to compare and contrast cultural dynamics at the societal, organizational and leadership levels to explore ways the leader reflects or diverges from broader cultural elements in his or her own rhetoric. In a similar fashion, archival data has been used to understand the process of how amateurs become perceived as legitimate experts by the larger public (see Croidieu & Kim, 2018). Similar explorations might be useful to better understand how leaders become to be perceived as legitimate in relation to culture to understand, for example, how leadership came to be constructed as a masculine profession and who contributed to that narrative? STM approaches can be applied to understand other ways in which particular aspects of culture are embedded in leadership. For example, scholars interested in leadership diversity might leverage STMs to understand how whiteness is engrained in leadership. More specifically, newspaper coverage, and social media data leading up to presidential elections involving minority candidates might lend insight into how audiences construct Whiteness or Blackness in association to leader discourse. Table 7 provides a more detailed look at potential topic model applications to leadership research questions.

In conclusion, we use a structural topic modeling approach to inductively mine a large corpus of text to identify the challenges leaders face. Our paper both informs the science and practice of leadership Appendix A. Means, standard deviations, and correlations Variable development by identifying how individual and contextual factors play a role in the challenges faced by leaders and assists leadership scholars are faced with the challenge of inductively coding large amounts of unstructured texts in order to answer their specific questions of interest. Ultimately, we hope that this paper helps to advance the study of leadership in the digital era.
